concepts.finding:
In summary, in this chapter, we studied thoroughly the eﬀectiveness
of relevance models to address the top-N recommendation task and
proposed improvements to the original proposal.
concepts.implication: Inform search ranking and retrieval optimization

concepts.finding:
In the next chapter, we
introduce a diﬀerent adaptation of relevance models to deal with a novel
recommendation problem.

in ranking accuracy, diversity and novelty on all the datasets except for

concepts.finding:
Our experiments showed that a uniform neighbor prior with the
probabilistic estimator based on Jelinek-Mercer smoothing for items can
signiﬁcantly improve the ranking accuracy, diversity and novelty of the
recommendation.
concepts.implication: Relevant for similarity-based retrieval and lookalike targeting

We adjusted co-
sine similarity with the ﬁndings of these analyses and proposed two
modiﬁed versions of the similarity that improves the original formulation
in terms of ranking accuracy, diversity and novelty.
concepts.implication: Relevant for similarity-based retrieval and lookalike targeting

concepts.finding:
We investigate the conventional top-N recommendation
task, but we also explore the recently formulated user-item group forma-
tion problem and propose a novel task based on the liquidation of long
tail items.
concepts.implication: Applicable to user/item clustering for personalization

propose the item-based counterpart of relevance models for CF showing
that it is an eﬀective algorithm for tackling a new task: getting rid of
long tail items with recommendations.

Finally, we conduct a series of experiments
that show that the use of a linear length prior for the users and a proba-
bilistic length prior based on Dirichlet smoothing for the items leads to
signiﬁcant improvements in terms of ranking accuracy.
concepts.implication: Inform search ranking and retrieval optimization

We propose a diﬀerent adaptation of relevance
models to deal with a novel recommendation task.
concepts.implication: Inform search ranking and retrieval optimization

concepts.finding:
6.2.1
Problem formulation
We propose a novel recommendation problem: instead of generating
the best item suggestions for each user, we aim to ﬁnd the best users
for each long tail product.
concepts.implication: General ML methodology applicable to content recommendation

concepts.finding:
In the next chapter, we use this item-based adaptation of relevance
models to address another novel recommendation problem.
concepts.implication: Inform search ranking and retrieval optimization

oracle that, although it shows a more modest performance, is more ap-
propriate for current grouping strategies which are based principally on
co-occurrences of ratings.
concepts.implication: Applicable to user/item clustering for personalization

concepts.finding:
Extensive experimen-
tation on four datasets conﬁrms that our new formulations of cosine
similarity generate neighborhoods that produce better recommendations
regarding ranking quality, diversity and novelty.

diversity and novelty at no extra cost—we do not need to add any new
parameter to the similarity measure.
